methodology_sections: 1. Lifecycle Integrity - State transitions, illegal states, predecessors
2. Monotonicity & Irreversibility - Increasing values, one-way transitions
3. Access & Permission Logic - Conditional access, role requirements
4. Time & Resource Integrity - Time-locks, resource bounds, expiry

invariants.description: burn() violates checks-effects-interactions: token transfers occur BEFORE liquidityBalances, totalSupply, and reserves are updated, enabling cross-function reentrancy. An attacker-controlled `to` contract receiving userToken can reenter executeFeeSwap (swapping the received userTokens) using stale, un-decremented reserveValidatorToken, extracting validatorTokens at the original rate before the burn's state update reduces them.
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker provides liquidity, then calls burn with `to` set to a malicious contract. During IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, ...), the malicious contract approves FeeAMM and reenters executeFeeSwap with the received userTokens. Since burn has not yet decremented reserveValidatorToken, the feeSwap succeeds against inflated reserves. The attacker extracts both burn proceeds AND additional validatorTokens from other LPs' reserves.
invariants.invariant_logic: For all external calls in burn(): liquidityBalances[poolId][msg.sender] and pool.reserve* must be updated BEFORE any external token transfers
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_burn_no_reentrancy() public view { /* Cannot be expressed as a pure state invariant; requires reentrancy guard: nonReentrant modifier on burn, executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap */ }

invariants.description: executeFeeSwap() performs IERC20(userToken).transferFrom BEFORE updating reserveUserToken and reserveValidatorToken. If userToken is a malicious/hookable token (ERC-777, ERC-4626, or custom hook), it can reenter executeFeeSwap. Since reserveValidatorToken has not been decremented, the sufficiency check passes repeatedly, allowing the attacker to drain all validatorToken reserves.
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker deploys a malicious ERC20 as userToken. Creates a pool via mint. Calls executeFeeSwap, and during transferFrom the malicious token reenters executeFeeSwap. The reserveValidatorToken check passes each time since state is not yet updated. Each reentrant call transfers out validatorTokens. The final state update only decrements for one call's worth, but multiple amountOut worth of validatorTokens have been sent.
invariants.invariant_logic: reserveValidatorToken -= amountOut MUST happen BEFORE IERC20(validatorToken).transfer(msg.sender, amountOut) AND before any external call including IERC20(userToken).transferFrom
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_feeswap_effects_before_interactions() public { /* Requires structural code change: move pool.reserveUserToken += and pool.reserveValidatorToken -= before the transferFrom call */ }

invariants.description: burn() also enables cross-function reentrancy into rebalanceSwap. During IERC20(validatorToken).transfer(to, ...) in burn, the attacker-controlled `to` contract can reenter rebalanceSwap to extract userTokens using stale (un-decremented) reserveUserToken. Combined with the first transfer reentrancy, both reserves can be drained beyond the LP's fair share.
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker's `to` contract receives validatorToken during burn, then reenters rebalanceSwap: deposits those validatorTokens and withdraws userTokens against the stale reserveUserToken. Since burn hasn't updated reserveUserToken yet, the rebalanceSwap check `pool.reserveUserToken < amountOut` passes for amounts beyond the attacker's share. The pool is drained of both token types.
invariants.invariant_logic: pool.reserveUserToken and pool.reserveValidatorToken must reflect all pending withdrawals BEFORE any external call in burn()
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_burn_cross_rebalance() public view { /* Requires nonReentrant guard across burn, rebalanceSwap, and executeFeeSwap */ }

invariants.description: All transfer() and transferFrom() calls lack return value checks (no SafeERC20). Tokens that return false instead of reverting on failure will cause state desynchronization: reserves are updated but actual token balances don't change. Especially dangerous in rebalanceSwap where state is updated BEFORE transfers.
invariants.attack_scenario: In rebalanceSwap: pool state (reserveValidatorToken+=, reserveUserToken-=) is updated, then IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom is called. If validatorToken returns false (e.g., insufficient allowance on a non-reverting token), the contract never received validatorTokens but has already decremented reserveUserToken and subsequently transfers out userTokens via IERC20(userToken).transfer. Attacker gets free userTokens.
invariants.invariant_logic: For all token operations: actualBalanceChange == expectedReserveChange; require(IERC20.transfer(...) == true) AND require(IERC20.transferFrom(...) == true)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_transfer_success() public view { /* Replace all transfer/transferFrom with SafeERC20.safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom */ }

invariants.description: Pool reserves must always reflect the actual token balances allocated to that pool. Fee-on-transfer tokens will cause reserves to exceed actual balances because the contract credits the full amountIn to reserves rather than the net amount received. Over time, the pool becomes insolvent — unable to honor all withdrawal/swap claims.
invariants.attack_scenario: A pool is created with a fee-on-transfer token as validatorToken (2% fee). LP deposits 10000 tokens, but only 9800 arrive. Reserve records 10000. When a feeSwap tries to send out amountOut based on reserveValidatorToken, the contract may not have sufficient balance, causing later burns or swaps to fail, trapping some LPs' funds.
invariants.invariant_logic: pool.reserveUserToken <= IERC20(userToken).balanceOf(address(this)) [per pool aggregate] AND pool.reserveValidatorToken <= IERC20(validatorToken).balanceOf(address(this)) [per pool aggregate]
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_reserve_solvency(address userToken, address validatorToken) public view { bytes32 pid = getPoolId(userToken, validatorToken); Pool memory p = pools[pid]; assert(p.reserveUserToken <= IERC20(userToken).balanceOf(address(this))); assert(p.reserveValidatorToken <= IERC20(validatorToken).balanceOf(address(this))); }

invariants.description: No slippage protection exists on any function. executeFeeSwap and rebalanceSwap have no minimum/maximum output parameters. burn has no minimum amountUserToken or amountValidatorToken parameters. Users are vulnerable to sandwich attacks and unfavorable execution when pool state changes between transaction submission and execution.
invariants.attack_scenario: An LP submits burn() to withdraw liquidity. Between submission and execution, an attacker performs a large rebalanceSwap that drains most reserveUserToken from the pool. When the LP's burn executes, they receive mostly validatorTokens and almost no userTokens. The attacker then re-enters the opposite swap to profit from the temporary imbalance.
invariants.invariant_logic: burn() should enforce: amountUserToken >= minUserTokenOut AND amountValidatorToken >= minValidatorTokenOut; executeFeeSwap should enforce: amountOut >= minAmountOut; rebalanceSwap should enforce: amountIn <= maxAmountIn
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_slippage_protection() public pure { /* Requires adding slippage parameters to function signatures and enforce with require statements */ }

invariants.description: mint() performs transferFrom before updating reserveValidatorToken, totalSupply, and liquidityBalances. If validatorToken has reentrant capabilities (ERC-777 tokensToSend hook), the callback can reenter mint with stale reserve and totalSupply values, receiving a more favorable liquidity calculation (lower denominator) and minting excess LP tokens at the expense of existing LPs.
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker uses an ERC-777 token as validatorToken. On first non-initial mint, the tokensToSend hook fires during transferFrom. The hook reenters mint(). Since reserveValidatorToken and totalSupply haven't been updated from the first call, denom is smaller and _totalSupply is smaller than they should be, but the ratio yields extra LP tokens when compounded across multiple reentrant deposits because the denominator should have grown from the first deposit.
invariants.invariant_logic: pool.reserveValidatorToken += amount MUST happen BEFORE any external call in mint(); totalSupply and liquidityBalances updates must also precede external calls
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_mint_effects_first() public { /* Requires reordering: compute liquidity, update all state, THEN transferFrom */ }

invariants.description: totalSupply[poolId] should never drop below MIN_LIQUIDITY once the pool is initialized. If it could, the pool could be re-initialized via mint's first-deposit branch, double-adding MIN_LIQUIDITY and corrupting the LP share calculation. The MIN_LIQUIDITY tokens are permanently locked (not assigned to any address) and must remain in totalSupply.
invariants.attack_scenario: If a bug or rounding allowed totalSupply to reach 0, a subsequent mint would trigger the first-deposit branch, adding another MIN_LIQUIDITY to totalSupply without properly accounting for existing reserves (which may be non-zero due to rounding dust). This would dilute existing locked liquidity and corrupt the share price for new depositors.
invariants.invariant_logic: For all poolIds where pool was initialized: totalSupply[poolId] >= MIN_LIQUIDITY; once totalSupply > 0 for a pool, the first-deposit branch (reserveUserToken == 0 && reserveValidatorToken == 0) should not be reachable
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_min_liquidity_locked(address userToken, address validatorToken) public view { bytes32 pid = getPoolId(userToken, validatorToken); if (totalSupply[pid] > 0) { assert(totalSupply[pid] >= MIN_LIQUIDITY); } }

invariants.description: executeFeeSwap and rebalanceSwap do not validate that userToken != validatorToken. While mint blocks identical addresses, swaps on a pool created with valid distinct tokens could be called with swapped token arguments (using a different poolId). This could create ghost pools with reserves that reference the wrong tokens, causing token accounting corruption if the same token address appears as both userToken in one pool and validatorToken in another.
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker calls executeFeeSwap(tokenA, tokenA, amount). The poolId is keccak256(tokenA, tokenA). No pool exists so reserveValidatorToken == 0, which causes revert. However, if a pool with reserves was somehow constructed for identical tokens via state manipulation (e.g., through reentrancy during mint where the identical address check was bypassed), funds could be stolen. The missing check increases attack surface.
invariants.invariant_logic: All swap functions should enforce: userToken != validatorToken
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_no_identical_tokens_in_swap(address userToken, address validatorToken) public pure { assert(userToken != validatorToken); }

invariants.description: The constant-multiplier pricing (amountOut = amountIn * M / SCALE) means the pool does not use a bonding curve and has no price impact. A single large swap pays the same rate as many small swaps. This creates a guaranteed arbitrage whenever market prices diverge from the fixed rate, as there is no increasing cost for large trades. LPs bear all impermanent loss with no compensating mechanism.
invariants.attack_scenario: If the external market price of userToken/validatorToken deviates from the fixed 0.997 rate, arbitrageurs can drain the favorable side of every pool until reserves are exhausted. LPs cannot withdraw in time because there is no slippage protection and the constant rate provides no price discovery. All value is extracted by arbitrageurs.
invariants.invariant_logic: LP value: forall pool: LP_share_value(t1) >= LP_share_value(t0) adjusted for fees [this invariant is violated by design when market prices move]
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_lp_value_preservation() public view { /* Cannot be enforced without oracle or bonding curve; design-level issue */ }
